plan.contextChunk:
also have the same horizon line. In @fig-palma_horizon, the lines in
the building facade that are parallel to the ground also converge to a
vanishing point that lies on the horizon line. In this particular
examples, the lines in the facade also happen to be parallel to one of
the axis of the floor tiles, converging to the same vanishing point on
the right side of the figure.

The ground plane is a special plane because it supports most objects.
This is different than a wall. Both are planes, but the ground has an
ecological importance that a wall does not have. The point of contact
between objects and the ground plane informs about its distance. The
distance between the contact point and the horizon line is related to
distance between the object and the camera. Objects further away have a
contact point projecting onto a higher point in the image plane.

@fig-horizon_line_cross_products illustrates how to detect the horizon
line using homogeneous coordinates. If lines $\boldsymbol{l}_1$ and
$\boldsymbol{l}_2$, in homogeneous coordinates, correspond to the 2D
projection of 3D parallel lines, we can obtain the vanishing point as
their intersection (cross-product,
$\boldsymbol{l}_1 \times \boldsymbol{l}_2$). If we now have another set
of parallel lines, contained in the same plane, $\boldsymbol{l}_3$ and
$\boldsymbol{l}_4$, we can get another vanishing point by computing
their cross-product. Finally, the horizon line is the line that passes
through both points that can be computed as the cross-product of the
vanishing points in homogeneous coordinates.

![Using parallel lines, in homogeneous coordinates, to compute vanishing points and the horizon line.](figures/imaging_geometry/horizon_line.png){width="90%" #fig-horizon_line_cross_products}

### Detecting Vanishing Points

@fig-vanishing_lines shows the office scene and three vanishing points
computed manually. In this scene there are three dominant orientations
along orthogonal directions. The three vanishing points are estimated by
selecting pairs of long edges in the image corresponding to parallel 3D
lines in the scene and finding their intersection.

![Most object boundaries in this photograph are oriented parallel to one of the world-coordinates reference frame (the world-coordinates frame is chosen in this way on purpose). All the parallel 3D lines converge on the image plane in a vanishing point. In this picture there are three main vanishing points, each of them related to one of the world-coordinates frame.](figures/single_view_3d/office_vanishing_points_3.png){width="100%" #fig-vanishing_lines}

Vanishing points can be extracted automatically from images by first
detecting image edges and their orientations (like we did in chapter
@sec-simplesystem) and then using RANSAC (@sec-RANSAC) to group the edges: every pair of edges will
vote for a vanishing point and the point with the largest support will

elements: tan ground plane tiled into a grid
sky-blue background above the horizon
red horizon line across the upper third of the image
four thick black lines (l1, l2, l3, l4) on the ground plane with labels
thinner lines extending from each thick line to vanishing points
two green vanishing points as spheres on the horizon line, one for l1/l2, one for l3/l4
math labels at each vanishing point: l1×l2 and l3×l4
horizon line label: h = (l1×l2)×(l3×l4) above line
intersection points and cross-product notation rendered as floating LaTeX

interactions.label: Show horizon line
interactions.teaches: Toggles the visibility of the red horizon line, emphasizing its meaning as the locus of vanishing points for lines in the ground plane

interactions.label: Vanishing point separation
interactions.teaches: Controls the spacing between the two vanishing points, corresponding to different camera orientations or scenes with varying perspective strength

interactions.label: Ground tilt
interactions.teaches: Tilts the ground plane up or down, making the horizon rise or fall to show how ground orientation affects vanishing point positions

demo_steps.title: All lines meet at vanishing points
demo_steps.narration: Thick black lines on the ground represent two sets of parallel lines in 3D. In the image, each set appears to converge at a separate vanishing point on the horizon. These vanishing points, shown as green dots, are the cross-products of corresponding homogeneous line equations.

demo_steps.title: Perspective strength: vanishing points move
demo_steps.narration: Increase the separation between vanishing points. This simulates a wider field of view or stronger perspective: grid lines appear to converge faster, and the vanishing points move further apart on the horizon. In real images, more distant vanishing points mean weaker perspective.

demo_steps.title: Horizon line: locus of vanishing points
demo_steps.narration: Hide the horizon line for a moment and notice the green vanishing points seem to float in space. Now toggle it back on: see that both vanishing points always lie exactly on this red line, no matter how they move. The horizon line is computed as the cross-product of the vanishing points and represents all possible vanishing points for lines on the ground plane.

demo_steps.title: Ground tilt affects horizon height
demo_steps.narration: Tilt the ground plane up by 20 degrees. The grid now appears steeper and the horizon rises in the frame, making the vanishing points move higher. This models how camera orientation or sloped ground changes the apparent position of the horizon and vanishing points.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Wide-angle view from above ground level, looking toward the horizon, so both ground plane and vanishing points on the horizon are visible. Perspective camera to enhance the effect of convergence.
interactionPlan.notes: Render lines in homogeneous form so their intersection and cross-product are accurate even if the scene is extended. Use strong color contrast and bold lines for clarity. Animate vanishing point positions and ground tilt smoothly to maximize pedagogical impact.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The brown ground color is paler in the generated figure than the original, making the black lines less visually strong.
L1, L2, L3, L4 label positions are slightly off and smaller than in the source, which uses bold large font centered over the lines.
The grid lines in the generated version extend further and are more numerous/denser than in the original figure.
There are extra UI controls, step narration, and a blue highlight box that don't exist in the original static figure.
The perspective/tilt controls in the UI introduce interactive states not presented in the source image (which is single state).
evaluation.notes: Faithfully renders main primitives and vanishing points with excellent interactivity, but grid density, palette, and some label placements diverge from the original.
action_items: Increase the font size and boldness of the L1-L4 labels and reposition them closer to match the original image positions.
Reduce the density and extension of grid lines so the scene less visually busy and better matches the source.
Darken the ground plane to more closely match the saturation/contrast of the original, making the black lines pop stronger.
Allow an option to hide UI overlays for a static, textbook-style rendering.
Add a 'reset to textbook' button/state to match the original figure exactly.

feedback.rationale: All issues are execution-level (label sizing/placement, grid density, palette mismatch, unnecessary UI elements), with no conceptual or planning errors indicated, so generator refinement is appropriate.
action_items: Increase the font size and boldness of the L1-L4 labels and reposition them closer to match the original image positions.
Reduce the density and extension of grid lines so the scene less visually busy and better matches the source.
Darken the ground plane to more closely match the saturation/contrast of the original, making the black lines pop stronger.
Allow an option to hide UI overlays for a static, textbook-style rendering.
Add a 'reset to textbook' button/state to match the original figure exactly.
actionItems: Increase the font size and boldness of the L1-L4 labels and reposition them closer to match the original image positions.
Reduce the density and extension of grid lines so the scene less visually busy and better matches the source.
Darken the ground plane to more closely match the saturation/contrast of the original, making the black lines pop stronger.
Allow an option to hide UI overlays for a static, textbook-style rendering.
Add a 'reset to textbook' button/state to match the original figure exactly.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The original has no interactive UI or step narration visible in the diagram region.
The original figure has only labels near the horizon and on the thick lines; the generated result introduces more step/UI-related texts.
Some thin grid lines in the generated figure overshoot the boundaries shown in the original and are more numerous.
The horizon line in the generated is implemented interactively, rather than statically as in the original figure.
Font size and exact placement of mathematical labels are close, but not exact to the original—they are slightly more distant from the vanishing points.
evaluation.notes: Faithfully captures the geometry, vanishing point structure, and mathematical labels with robust, pedagogically strong interactions; minor UI and label placement differences.
action_items: Fine-tune mathematical label placement and font size to more closely match original figure's positions and scale.
Refine number and range of thin grid lines so none extend far beyond the figure's bounds as in the source.
Optionally hide interactive controls by default, only showing them on request to maximize visual fidelity for static screenshots.

feedback.rationale: All core conceptual elements are present and the only remaining issues are minor aesthetic and UI discrepancies, which do not warrant another iteration since the fidelity and educational value are already very high.
action_items: Fine-tune mathematical label placement and font size to more closely match original figure's positions and scale.

Optionally hide interactive controls by default, only showing them on request to maximize visual fidelity for static screenshots.
